Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd have these bursts where I'd tell myself I was going to get organized, but then I'd just drift through the week without much structure. I usually ended up studying late at night when anxiety kicked in, scrolling through materials without a real plan. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks I barely touched anything until something felt urgent.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly driven by deadlines or when my anxiety spiked about falling behind. I never really established a consistent weekly schedule even though I have the time and resources to do so. Sometimes I'd study intensively for a few days, then basically disappear from coursework for a while. It's frustrating because I know I could do better if I just stuck to something regular.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or this nagging anxious feeling that I was missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were new announcements, then end up clicking around without really accomplishing much. The mindfulness exercises they suggested actually helped me recognize when I was logging in out of panic versus genuine intention to study.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in daily for maybe a week, but it never became automatic for me. I'd get surprised by how much time had passed since my last login sometimes. The habit just never stuck, even though I kept telling myself it would eventually click into place.
Q5: The visual materials were definitely most useful for me—diagrams, infographics, video lectures where I could see concepts illustrated. I learn best when I can actually see what's being explained rather than just reading dense text. The videos were good because I could pause and replay parts that confused me, which helped manage my anxiety about not understanding things immediately.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time, even though I posted occasionally. Reading through long text-based discussions felt overwhelming and I'd just skim past them. The supplementary readings also piled up—I kept downloading them thinking I'd get to them later, but rarely did. It felt like too much effort for uncertain payoff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams, or when it was presented in a visually engaging way. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I'd actually be assessed on, I'd lose interest quickly. I also appreciated when activities were shorter and more focused rather than these long, sprawling modules.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I'd feel guilty about not doing them, which added to my anxiety, but I also couldn't motivate myself to actually complete them. Required ones at least had the pressure of consequences, so I'd eventually get them done. Optional stuff just sat there making me feel bad about myself without any real push to engage.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and lecture slides early on and would study from those on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I also watched some YouTube videos that explained concepts better than our course materials sometimes. My notes app has tons of stuff that I worked through offline.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my downloaded slides or rewatching videos I'd saved locally, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also spend time just thinking through problems or concepts while doing other things—like working through ideas during walks. The LMS only sees when I'm clicking around, not when I'm actually processing information.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF, watch or read it once online to make sure it downloaded properly, then study it repeatedly offline. My actual engagement with that material was way deeper than one view would suggest. I preferred studying offline because it felt less overwhelming than being in the LMS with all those notifications and unfinished tasks staring at me.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My anxiety was really high and I felt paralyzed by how much had accumulated. I'd log in, see all the incomplete tasks, feel overwhelmed, and just close the browser. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit, but my mood stayed pretty low. I was surprised by how quickly I'd fallen behind without really noticing.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one—I'd be too mentally exhausted to engage meaningfully even when I had time. My anxiety made it hard to concentrate, so I'd end up just clicking through things without absorbing much. I don't have competing work or financial pressures, which almost made it worse because I couldn't blame external factors for my disengagement.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have the LMS open, technically watching a video or scrolling through readings, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd finish a lecture and realize I couldn't remember anything I'd just watched. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety of starting something and potentially doing it wrong felt worse than the anxiety of not doing it at all, if that makes sense. I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's a cycle I haven't figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have these intense last-minute sessions where I'd finally do what I'd been avoiding for weeks. My assignment scores were actually decent because I'd hyperfocus right before due dates, but the process was stressful and unsustainable. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have completed much at all.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. Good feedback felt surprising and made me think maybe I could do this after all. Critical feedback, even when constructive, would trigger my anxiety and make me avoid that topic or assignment type afterward. I know that's not a healthy response, but it's honest.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and options. I actually would have preferred a more rigid, linear path telling me exactly what to do and when. The flexibility that's supposed to be helpful just gave me more opportunities to drift and fall behind. I needed more guardrails than the course provided.
Q19: The visual resources made engagement easier—anything with diagrams or video demonstrations. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear prioritization. I couldn't tell what was essential versus nice-to-have, so I'd either try to do everything and burn out, or do almost nothing because I couldn't decide where to start.
Q20: That sounds familiar, honestly. I think sometimes you're trying to engage, clicking around, starting things, but never quite finishing because something feels off or overwhelming. High activity might mean someone is searching for something that helps them learn, without finding it. Or they're anxious and checking compulsively without making real progress.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam score was decent and I got Merit, but my LMS activity was pretty inconsistent. I think it's because I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials, and when I did engage, I engaged pretty deeply even if briefly. The LMS doesn't capture quality of engagement, just quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when someone's active, how often they log in, what they click on. But they miss the depth of engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They also miss all the offline work, the thinking, the anxiety that prevents engagement even when you want to engage.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling mentally present while doing it. It's not just completing tasks or logging hours—it's being curious, making connections, feeling like the material matters somehow. I've had moments of real engagement this semester, but they were scattered among a lot of going-through-the-motions.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much that affected my participation. It wouldn't show the offline studying I did, or the mental effort of trying to engage when my brain was fighting against it. It also wouldn't capture the surprise I felt at actually doing okay on exams despite feeling so disconnected most of the time. There's a whole internal experience that data just can't measure.
